glossaryTerms.definition: A hand-building technique where flat sheets of clay are rolled out and joined to form walls, bases, and other flat surfaces.
The standard method for joining two pieces of clay: scratch the surfaces to be joined, apply liquid clay (slip) to both, then press firmly together.

The stage of drying when clay is firm but still slightly damp - it can be handled without distorting and carved cleanly. For air-dry clay this is typically 1-3 hours after working, depending on humidity.
Coating a finished air-dry clay piece with undiluted PVA (polyvinyl acetate) glue. PVA dries clear and hard, adding surface strength and light moisture resistance.
A hole pierced in the base of a clay pot or planter to allow excess water to drain away and prevent waterlogged roots.

content.text: This slab-built tray holds three small pots for kitchen herbs on a windowsill. It is made entirely from flat
slab building
with air-dry clay and no equipment beyond a rolling pin and a knife. The three sections each have a
drainage hole
pierced through the base. The inside and base are sealed with neat
PVA sealing

to give light moisture resistance - this does not make it waterproof, but it is enough to hold a damp herb pot without the clay dissolving. Place a small saucer under each pot section when watering to be safe. The tray is a good first
score and slip
project: all joins are straight and visible, so errors are easy to spot and fix.

materials.name: Slip (air-dry clay mixed with water to a cream)

content.text: Preparation

Work out the tray dimensions before cutting any clay. Three small herb pots typically sit in a 10 x 10 cm footprint each, so the tray base should be roughly 30 x 12 cm (allowing 1 cm margin at each end and a 1 cm divider wall between each section). The walls are 5 cm tall. Sketch the layout on paper and mark all the piece dimensions: base slab (30 x 12 cm), two long wall strips (30 x 5 cm), two short end wall strips (12 x 5 cm), and two short divider strips (10 x 5 cm).
Make slip before rolling clay: mix a pinch of air-dry clay with water to a cream consistency. Keep it covered while working.
Steps

Roll out a slab to 6-7 mm thickness. Use two guide sticks of equal height on either side of the clay to ensure an even slab. Cut all seven pieces at once using the fettling knife and a ruler: the base, two long walls, two short end walls, and two dividers.
Allow the slabs to firm up to
leather hard
before joining. This takes 30-60 minutes depending on room temperature. Leather-hard slabs hold their shape when handled; fresh-rolled slabs will sag and distort.

Pierce the drainage holes before assembly. Hold the base slab flat and use the needle tool to pierce one hole in the centre of each of the three pot sections (mark their positions with a light pencil line first). Make each hole 5-6 mm in diameter. Air-dry clay shrinks slightly as it dries, so a slightly larger hole prevents it closing up.

Attach the two long walls first. Score the bottom edge of each long wall strip and the corresponding outer edges of the base slab. Apply slip to both scored surfaces. Press the wall firmly onto the base, holding for 10 seconds. Check that the inside angle is at 90 degrees. Blend the inside join seam with a damp finger.

Attach the two short end walls. Score all joining surfaces (base and the two touching long-wall ends). Apply slip and press into place. Blend all inside seams. The four outer walls should now stand as a complete rectangle.

Fit the two divider walls. Measure and mark their positions (roughly equal thirds of the interior). Score all contact surfaces: the bottom edge of each divider, the base, and the two long wall inner faces where the divider meets them. Apply slip and press in. Blend all seams from inside.

Smooth the exterior surfaces with a damp sponge while the clay is still soft. Run the sponge along each wall to remove fingerprints and level the surface. Leave the outside edge seams with a slightly rounded bead rather than a sharp right angle - it looks cleaner and is less likely to chip.
Let the tray dry for 48-3 days before handling. Check joins after 12 hours: if any gap has opened, press fresh slip into it while still slightly damp and smooth over.
Drying and finishing
Once fully bone-dry, sand lightly and paint with acrylics if wanted. Then apply
PVA sealing

: brush two coats of undiluted PVA onto the inside faces of the tray (all walls and base) and the underside of the base, allowing each coat to dry fully between coats. The PVA layer gives light moisture resistance and prevents the clay softening when a damp pot sits inside. It does not make the tray watertight for direct watering.
Variations
Add a pressed leaf or stamp texture to the outer walls before the clay firms - press firmly while fresh, lift cleanly.
Build the tray with two sections instead of three for larger pot varieties.
Add small clay feet (four short cylinder offcuts) to lift the base off the windowsill for drainage clearance.
Write herb names on the divider walls using a needle tool while leather-hard for a permanent label.

items.problem: Wall pulled away from base during drying
items.cause: Slabs were joined too wet, or slip was not applied to both scored surfaces
items.fix: Let slabs reach leather-hard before joining, and apply slip to both halves of every join.

items.problem: Base warped upward in the centre
items.cause: Base slab dried faster than the walls, pulling the clay
items.fix: Dry on a flat board with a light weight on the base (a piece of baking paper under the weight to prevent sticking).

items.problem: Cracks at the inside corner of each wall/base join
items.cause: Inner seam was not blended - a sharp inside angle concentrates stress
items.fix: Always blend inside seams with a damp finger to leave a slightly rounded inner radius.

items.problem: Drainage holes closed up during drying
items.cause: Holes were made too small and clay shrinkage sealed them
items.fix: Drill open with the needle tool before fully dry, or pierce larger (6-8 mm) next time.
